content.problemStatement:
You are given 2 arrays **W** =  (**W1**, **W2**, .., **WN**) and **C** =  (**C1**, **C2**, .., **CN**) with **N** elements each. A range [l, r] is unique if all the elements **Cl**, **Cl+1**, .., **Cr** are unique (ie. no duplicates). The sum of the range is **Wl** +  **Wl+1** + ... + **Wr**.

You want to find an unique range with the maximum sum possible, and output this sum.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 1000000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N. Second line contains N space‑separated integers C1 … CN (0 ≤ Ci < N). Third line contains N space‑separated integers W1 … WN (0 ≤ Wi ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of a contiguous sub‑array whose C‑values are all distinct.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The best unique sub‑array is indices 1‑3 (0‑based) with C = [1,2,0] and sum W = 6+7+8 = 21.
All three elements are distinct, so the whole array gives sum 1+2+3 = 6.
All C values are unique; taking the whole array yields the maximum sum 1+2+3+4 = 10.

solution.approach: We need the maximum sum of a sub‑array where the colour identifiers C are all distinct. This is a classic sliding‑window (two‑pointer) problem. Maintain a left pointer of the current window and a map/array last[] storing the last index where each colour appeared. While extending the right pointer, if the current colour was seen inside the current window (last[col] >= left) we move left just past that previous occurrence, subtracting the weights of the elements we discard. Simultaneously keep the running sum of the window and update the answer with the maximum sum seen. Because each element is added and removed at most once, the algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(N) extra memory for the last‑occurrence array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the last occurrence array (per test case)
